sentences.text: This section provides more explanation for RVWMO , using more informal language and concrete examples.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material

sentences.text: These are intended to clarify the meaning and intent of the axioms and preserved program order rules.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material

sentences.text: This appendix should be treated as commentary; all normative material is provided in and in the rest of the main body of the ISA specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material

sentences.text: All currently known discrepancies are listed in .
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material

sentences.text: Any other discrepancies are unintentional.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material

sentences.text: Memory consistency models fall along a loose spectrum from weak to strong.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: Weak memory models allow more hardware implementation flexibility and deliver arguably better performance, performance per watt, power, scalability, and hardware verification overheads than strong models, at the expense of a more complex programming model.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: Strong models provide simpler programming models, but at the cost of imposing more restrictions on the kinds of (non-speculative) hardware optimizations that can be performed in the pipeline and in the memory system, and in turn imposing some cost in terms of power, area overhead, and verification burden.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: RISC-V has chosen the RVWMO memory model, a variant of release consistency.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: This places it in between the two extremes of the memory model spectrum.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: The RVWMO memory model enables architects to build simple implementations, aggressive implementations, implementations embedded deeply inside a much larger system and subject to complex memory system interactions, or any number of other possibilities, all while simultaneously being strong enough to support programming language memory models at high performance.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: To facilitate the porting of code from other architectures, some hardware implementations may choose to implement the Ztso extension, which provides stricter RVTSO ordering semantics by default.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: Code written for RVWMO is automatically and inherently compatible with RVTSO, but code written assuming RVTSO is not guaranteed to run correctly on RVWMO implementations.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: In fact, most RVWMO implementations will (and should) simply refuse to run RVTSO-only binaries.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: Each implementation must therefore choose whether to prioritize compatibility with RVTSO code (e.g., to facilitate porting from x86) or whether to instead prioritize compatibility with other RISC-V cores implementing RVWMO.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: Some fences and/or memory ordering annotations in code written for RVWMO may become redundant under RVTSO; the cost that the default of RVWMO imposes on Ztso implementations is the incremental overhead of fetching those fences (e.g., FENCE R,RW and FENCE RW,W) which become no-ops on that implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: However, these fences must remain present in the code if compatibility with non-Ztso implementations is desired.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

sentences.text: In this section, we provide explanation and examples for all of the RVWMO rules and axioms.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules

sentences.text: Preserved program order represents the subset of program order that must be respected within the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: Conceptually, events from the same hart that are ordered by preserved program order must appear in that order from the perspective of other harts and/or observers.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: Events from the same hart that are not ordered by preserved program order, on the other hand, may appear reordered from the perspective of other harts and/or observers.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: Informally, the global memory order represents the order in which loads and stores perform.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: The formal memory model literature has moved away from specifications built around the concept of performing, but the idea is still useful for building up informal intuition. A load is said to have performed when its return value is determined. A store is said to have performed not when it has executed inside the pipeline, but rather only when its value has been propagated to globally visible memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: In this sense, the global memory order also represents the contribution of the coherence protocol and/or the rest of the memory system to interleave the (possibly reordered) memory accesses being issued by each hart into a single total order agreed upon by all harts.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: The order in which loads perform does not always directly correspond to the relative age of the values those two loads return.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: In particular, a load b may perform before another load a to the same address (i.e., b may execute before a, and b may appear before a in the global memory order), but a may nevertheless return an older value than b.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: This discrepancy captures (among other things) the reordering effects of buffering placed between the core and memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: For example, b may have returned a value from a store in the store buffer, while a may have ignored that younger store and read an older value from memory instead.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: To account for this, at the time each load performs, the value it returns is determined by the load value axiom, not just strictly by determining the most recent store to the same address in the global memory order, as described below.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

sentences.text: [IMPORTANT] : Each byte of each load i returns the value written to that byte by the store that is the latest in global memory order among the following stores:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Stores that write that byte and that precede i in the global memory order .
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Stores that write that byte and that precede i in program order
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Preserved program order is not required to respect the ordering of a store followed by a load to an overlapping address.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: This complexity arises due to the ubiquity of store buffers in nearly all implementations.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Informally, the load may perform (return a value) by forwarding from the store while the store is still in the store buffer, and hence before the store itself performs (writes back to globally visible memory).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Any other hart will therefore observe the load as performing before the store.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: When running this program on an implementation with store buffers, it is possible to arrive at the final outcome `a0=1, a1=0, a2=1, a3=0` as follows:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: .A store buffer forwarding litmus test (outcome permitted) [float="center",align="center",cols=".^1a,.^1a",frame="none",grid="none",options="noheader"] | | ! 2+^!Hart 0 2+^!Hart 1 2+^!li t1, 1 2+^!li t1, 1 2+<!(a) sw t1,0(s0) 2+!(e) sw t1,0(s1) 2+<!(b) lw a0,0(s0) 2+!(f) lw a2,0(s1) 2+<!(c) fence r,r 2+!(g) fence r,r 2+<!(d) lw a1,0(s1) 2+!(h) lw a3,0(s0) 4+^!Outcome: `a0=1`, `a1=0`, `a2=1`, `a3=0` ! | ! //a! graphviz::images/graphviz/litmussbfwd.txt[] a! image::graphviz/litmussbfwd.png[] ! |
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: (a) executes and enters the first hart's private store buffer * (b) executes and forwards its return value 1 from (a) in the store buffer * (c) executes since all previous loads (i.e., (b)) have completed * (d) executes and reads the value 0 from memory * (e) executes and enters the second hart's private store buffer * (f) executes and forwards its return value 1 from (e) in the store buffer * (g) executes since all previous loads (i.e., (f)) have completed * (h) executes and reads the value 0 from memory * (a) drains from the first hart's store buffer to memory * (e) drains from the second hart's store buffer to memory
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Therefore, the memory model must be able to account for this behavior.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: To put it another way, suppose the definition of preserved program order did include the following hypothetical rule: memory access a precedes memory access b in preserved program order (and hence also in the global memory order) if a precedes b in program order and a and b are accesses to the same memory location, a is a write, and b is a read.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Then we get the following:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: (a) precedes (b): by rule X * (b) precedes (d): by rule * (d) precedes (e): by the load value axiom.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Otherwise, if (e) preceded (d), then (d) would be required to return the value 1. (This is a perfectly legal execution; it's just not the one in question) * (e) precedes (f): by rule X * (f) precedes (h): by rule * (h) precedes (a): by the load value axiom, as above.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: The global memory order must be a total order and cannot be cyclic, because a cycle would imply that every event in the cycle happens before itself, which is impossible.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Therefore, the execution proposed above would be forbidden, and hence the addition of rule X would forbid implementations with store buffer forwarding, which would clearly be undesirable.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Nevertheless, even if (b) precedes (a) and/or (f) precedes (e) in the global memory order, the only sensible possibility in this example is for (b) to return the value written by (a), and likewise for (f) and (e).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: This combination of circumstances is what leads to the second option in the definition of the load value axiom.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Even though (b) precedes (a) in the global memory order, (a) will still be visible to (b) by virtue of sitting in the store buffer at the time (b) executes.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Therefore, even if (b) precedes (a) in the global memory order, (b) should return the value written by (a) because (a) precedes (b) in program order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: Another test that highlights the behavior of store buffers is shown in .

sentences.text: In this example, (d) is ordered before (e) because of the control dependency, and (f) is ordered before (g) because of the address dependency.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: However, (e) is not necessarily ordered before (f), even though (f) returns the value written by (e).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: This could correspond to the following sequence of events:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: (e) executes speculatively and enters the second hart's private store buffer (but does not drain to memory) * (f) executes speculatively and forwards its return value 1 from (e) in the store buffer * (g) executes speculatively and reads the value 0 from memory * (a) executes, enters the first hart's private store buffer, and drains to memory * (b) executes and retires * (c) executes, enters the first hart's private store buffer, and drains to memory * (d) executes and reads the value 1 from memory * (e), (f), and (g) commit, since the speculation turned out to be correct * (e) drains from the store buffer to memory
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

sentences.text: [IMPORTANT] (for Aligned Atomics):
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: If r and w are paired load and store operations generated by aligned LR and SC instructions in a hart h, s is a store to byte x, and r returns a value written by s, then s must precede w in the global memory order, and there can be no store from a hart other than h to byte x following s and preceding w in the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: The RISC-V architecture decouples the notion of atomicity from the notion of ordering.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: Unlike architectures such as TSO, RISC-V atomics under RVWMO do not impose any ordering requirements by default.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: Ordering semantics are only guaranteed by the PPO rules that otherwise apply.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: RISC-V contains two types of atomics: AMOs and LR/SC pairs.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: These conceptually behave differently, in the following way. LR/SC behave as if the old value is brought up to the core, modified, and written back to memory, all while a reservation is held on that memory location. AMOs on the other hand conceptually behave as if they are performed directly in memory. AMOs are therefore inherently atomic, while LR/SC pairs are atomic in the slightly different sense that the memory location in question will not be modified by another hart during the time the original hart holds the reservation.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: [frame=none] .In all four (independent) instances, the final store-conditional instruction is permitted but not guaranteed to succeed. | |(a) lr.d a0, 0(s0) |(a) lr.d a0, 0(s0) |(a) lr.w a0, 0(s0) |(a) lr.w a0, 0(s0)
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom
Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom
Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom
Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: The atomicity axiom forbids stores from other harts from being interleaved in global memory order between an LR and the SC paired with that LR.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: The atomicity axiom does not forbid loads from being interleaved between the paired operations in program order or in the global memory order, nor does it forbid stores from the same hart or stores to non-overlapping locations from appearing between the paired operations in either program order or in the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: For example, the SC instructions in may (but are not guaranteed to) succeed.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: None of those successes would violate the atomicity axiom, because the intervening non-conditional stores are from the same hart as the paired load-reserved and store-conditional instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: This way, a memory system that tracks memory accesses at cache line granularity (and which therefore will see the four snippets of as identical) will not be forced to fail a store-conditional instruction that happens to (falsely) share another portion of the same cache line as the memory location being held by the reservation.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: The atomicity axiom also technically supports cases in which the LR and SC touch different addresses and/or use different access sizes; however, use cases for such behaviors are expected to be rare in practice.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: Likewise, scenarios in which stores from the same hart between an LR/SC pair actually overlap the memory location(s) referenced by the LR or SC are expected to be rare compared to scenarios where the intervening store may simply fall onto the same cache line.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

sentences.text: [IMPORTANT] : No memory operation may be preceded in the global memory order by an infinite sequence of other memory operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: The progress axiom ensures a minimal forward progress guarantee.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: It ensures that stores from one hart will eventually be made visible to other harts in the system in a finite amount of time, and that loads from other harts will eventually be able to read those values (or successors thereof).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: Without this rule, it would be legal, for example, for a spinlock to spin infinitely on a value, even with a store from another hart unlocking the spinlock.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: The progress axiom is intended not to impose any other notion of fairness, latency, or quality of service onto the harts in a RISC-V implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: Any stronger notions of fairness are up to the rest of the ISA and/or up to the platform and/or device to define and implement.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: The forward progress axiom will in almost all cases be naturally satisfied by any standard cache coherence protocol.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: Implementations with non-coherent caches may have to provide some other mechanism to ensure the eventual visibility of all stores (or successors thereof) to all harts.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

sentences.text: Same-address orderings where the latter is a store are straightforward: a load or store can never be reordered with a later store to an overlapping memory location.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: From a microarchitecture perspective, generally speaking, it is difficult or impossible to undo a speculatively reordered store if the speculation turns out to be invalid, so such behavior is simply disallowed by the model.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Same-address orderings from a store to a later load, on the other hand, do not need to be enforced.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: As discussed in , this reflects the observable behavior of implementations that forward values from buffered stores to later loads.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Same-address load-load ordering requirements are far more subtle.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: The basic requirement is that a younger load must not return a value that is older than a value returned by an older load in the same hart to the same address.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: This is often known as "CoRR" (Coherence for Read-Read pairs), or as part of a broader "coherence" or "sequential consistency per location" requirement.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Some architectures in the past have relaxed same-address load-load ordering, but in hindsight this is generally considered to complicate the programming model too much, and so RVWMO requires CoRR ordering to be enforced.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: However, because the global memory order corresponds to the order in which loads perform rather than the ordering of the values being returned, capturing CoRR requirements in terms of the global memory order requires a bit of indirection.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: .A litmus test MP+fence.w.w+fri-rfi-addr (outcome permitted)
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: [float="center",align="center",cols=".^1a,.^1a",frame="none",grid="none",options="noheader"] .Litmus test MP+fence.w.w+fre-rfi-addr (outcome permitted) | | ! 2+!Hart 0 2+^!Hart 1 !!li t1, 1 !!li t2, 2 >!(a) !sw t1,0(s0) >!(d) !lw a0,0(s1) >!(b) !fence w, w >!(e) !sw t2,0(s1) >!(c) !sw t1,0(s1) >!(f) !lw a1,0(s1) ! ! >!(g) !xor t3,a1,a1 ! ! >!(h) !add s0,s0,t3 ! ! >!(i) !lw a2,0(s0) 4+^!Outcome: `a0=1`, `a1=2`, `a2=0` ! | ! //a! graphviz::images/graphviz/litmusmpfencewwfrirfiaddr.txt[] a! image::graphviz/litmusmpfencewwfrirfiaddr.png[] ! | Consider the litmus test of , which is one particular instance of the more general "fri-rfi" pattern.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: The term "fri-rfi" refers to the sequence (d), (e), (f): (d) "from-reads" (i.e., reads from an earlier write than) (e) which is the same hart, and (f) reads from (e) which is in the same hart.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: From a microarchitectural perspective, outcome `a0=1`, `a1=2`, `a2=0` is legal (as are various other less subtle outcomes).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Intuitively, the following would produce the outcome in question:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: (d) stalls (for whatever reason; perhaps it's stalled waiting for some other preceding instruction) * (e) executes and enters the store buffer (but does not yet drain to memory) * (f) executes and forwards from (e) in the store buffer * (g), (h), and (i) execute * (a) executes and drains to memory, (b) executes, and (c) executes and drains to memory * (d) unstalls and executes * (e) drains from the store buffer to memory
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: This corresponds to a global memory order of (f), (i), (a), (c), (d), (e).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Note that even though (f) performs before (d), the value returned by (f) is newer than the value returned by (d).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Therefore, this execution is legal and does not violate the CoRR requirements.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Likewise, if two back-to-back loads return the values written by the same store, then they may also appear out-of-order in the global memory order without violating CoRR.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Note that this is not the same as saying that the two loads return the same value, since two different stores may write the same value.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: .Litmus test RSW (outcome permitted)
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: [float="center",align="center",cols=".^1a,.^1a",frame="none",grid="none",options="noheader"] | | ! 2+!Hart 0 2+^!Hart 1 2+!li t1, 1 >!(d) <!lw a0,0(s1) >!(a) <!sw t1,0(s0) >!(e) !xor t2,a0,a0 >!(b) <!fence w, w >!(f) !add s4,s2,t2 >!(c) <!sw t1,0(s1) >!(g) !lw a1,0(s4) ! ! >!(h) !lw a2,0(s2) ! ! >!(i) !xor t3,a2,a2 ! ! >!(j) !add s0,s0,t3 ! ! >!(k) !lw a3,0(s0) 4+!Outcome: `a0=1`, `a1=v`, `a2=v`, `a3=0` ! | ! //a! graphviz::images/graphviz/litmusrsw.txt[] a! image::graphviz/litmusrsw.png[] ! |
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Consider the litmus test of .
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: The outcome `a0=1`, `a1=v`, `a2=v`, `a3=0` (where v is some value written by another hart) can be observed by allowing (g) and (h) to be reordered.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: This might be done speculatively, and the speculation can be justified by the microarchitecture (e.g., by snooping for cache invalidations and finding none) because replaying (h) after (g) would return the value written by the same store anyway.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Hence assuming `a1` and `a2` would end up with the same value written by the same store anyway, (g) and (h) can be legally reordered.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: The global memory order corresponding to this execution would be (h),(k),(a),(c),(d),(g).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Executions of the test in in which `a1` does not equal `a2` do in fact require that (g) appears before (h) in the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Allowing (h) to appear before (g) in the global memory order would in that case result in a violation of CoRR, because then (h) would return an older value than that returned by (g).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Therefore, forbids this CoRR violation from occurring.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: As such, strikes a careful balance between enforcing CoRR in all cases while simultaneously being weak enough to permit "RSW" and "fri-rfi" patterns that commonly appear in real microarchitectures.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: There is one more overlapping-address rule: simply states that a value cannot be returned from an AMO or SC to a subsequent load until the AMO or SC has (in the case of the SC, successfully) performed globally.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: This follows somewhat naturally from the conceptual view that both AMOs and SC instructions are meant to be performed atomically in memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: However, notably, states that hardware may not even non-speculatively forward the value being stored by an AMOSWAP to a subsequent load, even though for AMOSWAP that store value is not actually semantically dependent on the previous value in memory, as is the case for the other AMOs.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: The same holds true even when forwarding from SC store values that are not semantically dependent on the value returned by the paired LR.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: The three PPO rules above also apply when the memory accesses in question only overlap partially.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: This can occur, for example, when accesses of different sizes are used to access the same object.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: Note also that the base addresses of two overlapping memory operations need not necessarily be the same for two memory accesses to overlap.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: When misaligned memory accesses are being used, the overlapping-address PPO rules apply to each of the component memory accesses independently.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

sentences.text: [IMPORTANT] Rule : There is a FENCE instruction that orders a before b
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: By default, the FENCE instruction ensures that all memory accesses from instructions preceding the fence in program order (the "predecessor set") appear earlier in the global memory order than memory accesses from instructions appearing after the fence in program order (the "successor set").
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: However, fences can optionally further restrict the predecessor set and/or the successor set to a smaller set of memory accesses in order to provide some speedup.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: Specifically, fences have PR, PW, SR, and SW bits which restrict the predecessor and/or successor sets.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: The predecessor set includes loads (resp.stores) if and only if PR (resp.PW) is set.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: Similarly, the successor set includes loads (resp.stores) if and only if SR (resp.SW) is set.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: The FENCE encoding currently has nine non-trivial combinations of the four bits PR, PW, SR, and SW, plus one extra encoding FENCE.TSO which facilitates mapping of "acquire+release" or RVTSO semantics.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: The remaining seven combinations have empty predecessor and/or successor sets and hence are no-ops.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: Of the ten non-trivial options, six are commonly used in practice:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: FENCE RW,RW * FENCE.TSO * FENCE RW,W * FENCE R,RW * FENCE R,R * FENCE W,W
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: FENCE instructions using other combinations of PR, PW, SR, and SW are not normally used in the Linux or C++ memory models but are otherwise well defined.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: Finally, we note that since RISC-V uses a multi-copy atomic memory model, programmers can reason about fences bits in a thread-local manner.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: Fences in RISC-V are not cumulative, as they are in some non-multi-copy-atomic memory models.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

sentences.text: [IMPORTANT] : a has an acquire annotation
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: : b has a release annotation
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: : a and b both have RCsc annotations
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: An acquire operation, as would be used at the start of a critical section, requires all memory operations following the acquire in program order to also follow the acquire in the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: This ensures, for example, that all loads and stores inside the critical section are up to date with respect to the synchronization variable being used to protect it.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Acquire ordering can be enforced in one of two ways: with an acquire annotation, which enforces ordering with respect to just the synchronization variable itself, or with a FENCE R,RW, which enforces ordering with respect to all previous loads.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: .A spinlock with atomics [source%linenums,asm] .... sd x1, (a1) Arbitrary unrelated store ld x2, (a2) Arbitrary unrelated load li t0, 1 Initialize swap value. again: amoswap.w.aq t0, t0, (a0) Attempt to acquire lock. bnez t0, again Retry if held. ...
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Critical section. ... amoswap.w.rl x0, x0, (a0) Release lock by storing 0. sd x3, (a3) Arbitrary unrelated store ld x4, (a4) Arbitrary unrelated load ....
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Because this example uses aq, the loads and stores in the critical section are guaranteed to appear in the global memory order after the AMOSWAP used to acquire the lock.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: However, assuming `a0`, `a1`, and `a2` point to different memory locations, the loads and stores in the critical section may or may not appear after the "Arbitrary unrelated load" at the beginning of the example in the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: .A spinlock with fences [source%linenums,asm] .... sd x1, (a1) Arbitrary unrelated store ld x2, (a2) Arbitrary unrelated load li t0, 1 Initialize swap value. again: amoswap.w t0, t0, (a0) Attempt to acquire lock. fence r, rw Enforce "acquire" memory ordering bnez t0, again Retry if held. ...
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Critical section. ... fence rw, w Enforce "release" memory ordering amoswap.w x0, x0, (a0) Release lock by storing 0. sd x3, (a3) Arbitrary unrelated store ld x4, (a4) Arbitrary unrelated load ....
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Now, consider the alternative in .
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: In this case, even though the AMOSWAP does not enforce ordering with an aq bit, the fence nevertheless enforces that the acquire AMOSWAP appears earlier in the global memory order than all loads and stores in the critical section.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Note, however, that in this case, the fence also enforces additional orderings: it also requires that the "Arbitrary unrelated load" at the start of the program appears earlier in the global memory order than the loads and stores of the critical section. (This particular fence does not, however, enforce any ordering with respect to the "Arbitrary unrelated store" at the start of the snippet.) In this way, fence-enforced orderings are slightly coarser than orderings enforced by .aq.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Release orderings work exactly the same as acquire orderings, just in the opposite direction.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Release semantics require all loads and stores preceding the release operation in program order to also precede the release operation in the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: This ensures, for example, that memory accesses in a critical section appear before the lock-releasing store in the global memory order.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Just as for acquire semantics, release semantics can be enforced using release annotations or with a FENCE RW,W operation.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

sentences.text: Instructions are said to carry dependencies from their source register(s) to their destination register(s) whenever the value written into each destination register is a function of the source register(s).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

sentences.text: For most instructions, this means that the destination register(s) carry a dependency from all source register(s).

sentences.text: However, there are a few notable exceptions.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

sentences.text: In the case of memory instructions, the value written into the destination register ultimately comes from the memory system rather than from the source register(s) directly, and so this breaks the chain of dependencies carried from the source register(s).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

sentences.text: In the case of unconditional jumps, the value written into the destination register comes from the current `pc` (which is never considered a source register by the memory model), and so likewise, JALR (the only jump with a source register) does not carry a dependency from rs1 to rd.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

sentences.text: In other words, this definition depends on the identities of the registers being accessed by different instructions, not the actual contents of those registers.

sentences.text: This means that an address, control, or data dependency must be enforced even if the calculation could seemingly be `optimized away`.

sentences.text: For example, there is a syntactic address dependency from the memory operation generated by the first instruction to the memory operation generated by the last instruction in , even though `a1` XOR `a1` is zero and hence has no effect on the address accessed by the second load.

sentences.text: One alternative would be to use a load-acquire, but this would enforce ordering for the first load with respect to all subsequent instructions.

sentences.text: Control dependencies behave differently from address and data dependencies in the sense that a control dependency always extends to all instructions following the original target in program order.

sentences.text: This definition of control dependency is subtly stronger than what might be seen in other contexts (e.g., C++), but it conforms with standard definitions of control dependencies in the literature.

sentences.text: Notably, PPO rules are also intentionally designed to respect dependencies that originate from the output of a successful store-conditional instruction.

sentences.text: Typically, an SC instruction will be followed by a conditional branch checking whether the outcome was successful; this implies that there will be a control dependency from the store operation generated by the SC instruction to any memory operations following the branch. PPO rule in turn implies that any subsequent store operations will appear later in the global memory order than the store operation generated by the SC.

sentences.text: However, since control, address, and data dependencies are defined over memory operations, and since an unsuccessful SC does not generate a memory operation, no order is enforced between unsuccessful SC and its dependent instructions.

sentences.text: Moreover, since SC is defined to carry dependencies from its source registers to rd only when the SC is successful, an unsuccessful SC has no effect on the global memory order.

sentences.text: RVWMO does not currently attempt to formally describe how FENCE.I, SFENCE.VMA, I/O fences, and PMAs behave.

sentences.text: All of these behaviors will be described by future formalizations.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory

sentences.text: In the meantime, the behavior of FENCE.I is described in , the behavior of SFENCE.VMA is described in the RISC-V Instruction Set Privileged Architecture Manual, and the behavior of I/O fences and the effects of PMAs are described below.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory

sentences.text: The RISC-V Privileged ISA defines Physical Memory Attributes (PMAs) which specify, among other things, whether portions of the address space are coherent and/or cacheable.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: See the RISC-V Privileged ISA Specification for the complete details.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: Here, we simply discuss how the various details in each PMA relate to the memory model:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: Main memory vs.I/O, and I/O memory ordering PMAs: the memory model as defined applies to main memory regions. I/O ordering is discussed below. * Supported access types and atomicity PMAs: the memory model is simply applied on top of whatever primitives each region supports. * Cacheability PMAs: the cacheability PMAs in general do not affect the memory model.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: Non-cacheable regions may have more restrictive behavior than cacheable regions, but the set of allowed behaviors does not change regardless.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: However, some platform-specific and/or device-specific cacheability settings may differ. * Coherence PMAs: The memory consistency model for memory regions marked as non-coherent in PMAs is currently platform-specific and/or device-specific: the load-value axiom, the atomicity axiom, and the progress axiom all may be violated with non-coherent memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: Note however that coherent memory does not require a hardware cache coherence protocol.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: The RISC-V Privileged ISA Specification suggests that hardware-incoherent regions of main memory are discouraged, but the memory model is compatible with hardware coherence, software coherence, implicit coherence due to read-only memory, implicit coherence due to only one agent having access, or otherwise. * Idempotency PMAs: Idempotency PMAs are used to specify memory regions for which loads and/or stores may have side effects, and this in turn is used by the microarchitecture to determine, e.g., whether prefetches are legal.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: This distinction does not affect the memory model.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

sentences.text: For I/O, the load value axiom and atomicity axiom in general do not apply, as both reads and writes might have device-specific side effects and may return values other than the value "written" by the most recent store to the same address.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Nevertheless, the following preserved program order rules still generally apply for accesses to I/O memory: memory access a precedes memory access b in global memory order if a precedes b in program order and one or more of the following holds:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: . a precedes b in preserved program order as defined in , with the exception that acquire and release ordering annotations apply only from one memory operation to another memory operation and from one I/O operation to another I/O operation, but not from a memory operation to an I/O nor vice versa . a and b are accesses to overlapping addresses in an I/O region . a and b are accesses to the same strongly ordered I/O region . a and b are accesses to I/O regions, and the channel associated with the I/O region accessed by either a or b is channel 1 . a and b are accesses to I/O regions associated with the same channel (except for channel 0)
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Note that the FENCE instruction distinguishes between main memory operations and I/O operations in its predecessor and successor sets.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: To enforce ordering between I/O operations and main memory operations, code must use a FENCE with PI, PO, SI, and/or SO, plus PR, PW, SR, and/or SW.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: For example, to enforce ordering between a write to main memory and an I/O write to a device register, a FENCE W,O or stronger is needed. .Ordering memory and I/O accesses [.text-center,source%linenums,asm] ---- sd t0, 0(a0) fence w,o sd a0, 0(a1) ----
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: When a fence is in fact used, implementations must assume that the device may attempt to access memory immediately after receiving the MMIO signal, and subsequent memory accesses from that device to memory must observe the effects of all accesses ordered prior to that MMIO operation.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: In other words, in , suppose `0(a0)` is in main memory and `0(a1)` is the address of a device register in I/O memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: If the device accesses `0(a0)` upon receiving the MMIO write, then that load must conceptually appear after the first store to `0(a0)` according to the rules of the RVWMO memory model.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: In some implementations, the only way to ensure this will be to require that the first store does in fact complete before the MMIO write is issued.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Other implementations may find ways to be more aggressive, while others still may not need to do anything different at all for I/O and main memory accesses.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Nevertheless, the RVWMO memory model does not distinguish between these options; it simply provides an implementation-agnostic mechanism to specify the orderings that must be enforced.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Many architectures include separate notions of "ordering" and "completion" fences, especially as it relates to I/O (as opposed to regular main memory).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Ordering fences simply ensure that memory operations stay in order, while completion fences ensure that predecessor accesses have all completed before any successors are made visible. RISC-V does not explicitly distinguish between ordering and completion fences.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Instead, this distinction is simply inferred from different uses of the FENCE bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: For implementations that conform to the RISC-V Unix Platform Specification, I/O devices and DMA operations are required to access memory coherently and via strongly ordered I/O channels.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Therefore, accesses to regular main memory regions that are concurrently accessed by external devices can also use the standard synchronization mechanisms.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Implementations that do not conform to the Unix Platform Specification and/or in which devices do not access memory coherently will need to use mechanisms (which are currently platform-specific or device-specific) to enforce coherency.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: I/O regions in the address space should be considered non-cacheable regions in the PMAs for those regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Such regions can be considered coherent by the PMA if they are not cached by any agent.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: The ordering guarantees in this section may not apply beyond a platform-specific boundary between the RISC-V cores and the device.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: In particular, I/O accesses sent across an external bus (e.g., PCIe) may be reordered before they reach their ultimate destination.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: Ordering must be enforced in such situations according to the platform-specific rules of those external devices and buses.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

sentences.text: provides a mapping from TSO memory operations onto RISC-V memory instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Normal x86 loads and stores are all inherently acquire-RCpc and release-RCpc operations: TSO enforces all load-load, load-store, and store-store ordering by default.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Therefore, under RVWMO, all TSO loads must be mapped onto a load followed by FENCE R,RW, and all TSO stores must be mapped onto FENCE RW,W followed by a store. TSO atomic read-modify-writes and x86 instructions using the LOCK prefix are fully ordered and can be implemented either via an AMO with both aq and rl set, or via an LR with aq set, the arithmetic operation in question, an SC with both aq and rl set, and a conditional branch checking the success condition.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: In the latter case, the rl annotation on the LR turns out (for non-obvious reasons) to be redundant and can be omitted.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Alternatives to are also possible. A TSO store can be mapped onto AMOSWAP with rl set.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: However, since RVWMO PPO Rule forbids forwarding of values from AMOs to subsequent loads, the use of AMOSWAP for stores may negatively affect performance. A TSO load can be mapped using LR with aq set: all such LR instructions will be unpaired, but that fact in and of itself does not preclude the use of LR for loads.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Since RISC-V does not currently have plain load and store opcodes with aq or rl annotations, ARM load-acquire and store-release operations should be mapped using fences instead.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Furthermore, in order to enforce store-release-to-load-acquire ordering, there must be a FENCE RW,RW between the store-release and load-acquire; enforces this by always placing the fence in front of each acquire operation. ARM load-exclusive and store-exclusive instructions can likewise map onto their RISC-V LR and SC equivalents, but instead of placing a FENCE RW,RW in front of an LR with aq set, we simply also set rl instead. ARM ISB maps on RISC-V to FENCE.I followed by FENCE R,R similarly to how ISYNC maps for Power.
