sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Any AMO can be emulated by an LR/SC pair, but care must be taken to ensure that any PPO orderings that originate from the LR are also made to originate from the SC, and that any PPO orderings that terminate at the SC are also made to terminate at the LR.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: For example, the LR must also be made to respect any data dependencies that the AMO has, given that load operations do not otherwise have any notion of a data dependency.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Likewise, the effect a FENCE R,R elsewhere in the same hart must also be made to apply to the SC, which would not otherwise respect that fence.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: The emulator may achieve this effect by simply mapping AMOs onto `lr.aq; <op>; sc.aqrl`, matching the mapping used elsewhere for fully ordered atomics.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: These C11/C++11 mappings require the platform to provide the following Physical Memory Attributes (as defined in the RISC-V Privileged ISA) for all memory:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: main memory * coherent * AMOArithmetic * RsrvEventual
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: Platforms with different attributes may require different mappings, or require platform-specific SW (e.g., memory-mapped I/O).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

sentences.text: The RVWMO and RVTSO memory models by no means preclude microarchitectures from employing sophisticated speculation techniques or other forms of optimization in order to deliver higher performance.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: The models also do not impose any requirement to use any one particular cache hierarchy, nor even to use a cache coherence protocol at all.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Instead, these models only specify the behaviors that can be exposed to software.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Microarchitectures are free to use any pipeline design, any coherent or non-coherent cache hierarchy, any on-chip interconnect, etc., as long as the design only admits executions that satisfy the memory model rules.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: That said, to help people understand the actual implementations of the memory model, in this section we provide some guidelines on how architects and programmers should interpret the models' rules.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Both RVWMO and RVTSO are multi-copy atomic (or other-multi-copy-atomic): any store value that is visible to a hart other than the one that originally issued it must also be conceptually visible to all other harts in the system.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: In other words, harts may forward from their own previous stores before those stores have become globally visible to all harts, but no early inter-hart forwarding is permitted.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Multi-copy atomicity may be enforced in a number of ways.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: It might hold inherently due to the physical design of the caches and store buffers, it may be enforced via a single-writer/multiple-reader cache coherence protocol, or it might hold due to some other mechanism.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Although multi-copy atomicity does impose some restrictions on the microarchitecture, it is one of the key properties keeping the memory model from becoming extremely complicated.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: For example, a hart may not legally forward a value from a neighbor hart's private store buffer (unless of course it is done in such a way that no new illegal behaviors become architecturally visible).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Nor may a cache coherence protocol forward a value from one hart to another until the coherence protocol has invalidated all older copies from other caches.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Of course, microarchitectures may (and high-performance implementations likely will) violate these rules under the covers through speculation or other optimizations, as long as any non-compliant behaviors are not exposed to the programmer.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: As a rough guideline for interpreting the PPO rules in RVWMO, we expect the following from the software perspective:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: programmers will use PPO rules and regularly and actively. * expert programmers will use PPO rules to speed up critical paths of important data structures. * even expert programmers will rarely if ever use PPO rules and directly.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: These are included to facilitate common microarchitectural optimizations (rule ) and the operational formal modeling approach (rules and ) described in .
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: They also facilitate the process of porting code from other architectures that have similar rules.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: We also expect the following from the hardware perspective:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: PPO rules and reflect well-understood rules that should pose few surprises to architects. * PPO rule reflects a natural and common hardware optimization, but one that is very subtle and hence is worth double checking carefully. * PPO rule may not be immediately obvious to architects, but it is a standard memory model requirement * The load value axiom, the atomicity axiom, and PPO rules reflect rules that most hardware implementations will enforce naturally, unless they contain extreme optimizations.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Of course, implementations should make sure to double check these rules nevertheless.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Hardware must also ensure that syntactic dependencies are not `optimized away`.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Architectures are free to implement any of the memory model rules as conservatively as they choose.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: For example, a hardware implementation may choose to do any or all of the following:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: interpret all fences as if they were FENCE RW,RW (or FENCE IORW,IORW, if I/O is involved), regardless of the bits actually set * implement all fences with PW and SR as if they were FENCE RW,RW (or FENCE IORW,IORW, if I/O is involved), as PW with SR is the most expensive of the four possible main memory ordering components anyway * emulate aq and rl as described in * enforcing all same-address load-load ordering, even in the presence of patterns such as `fri-rfi` and `RSW` * forbid any forwarding of a value from a store in the store buffer to a subsequent AMO or LR to the same address * forbid any forwarding of a value from an AMO or SC in the store buffer to a subsequent load to the same address * implement TSO on all memory accesses, and ignore any main memory fences that do not include PW and SR ordering (e.g., as Ztso implementations will do) * implement all atomics to be RCsc or even fully ordered, regardless of annotation
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Architectures that implement RVTSO can safely do the following:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Ignore all fences that do not have both PW and SR (unless the fence also orders I/O) * Ignore all PPO rules except for rules through , since the rest are redundant with other PPO rules under RVTSO assumptions
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Silent stores (i.e., stores that write the same value that already exists at a memory location) behave like any other store from a memory model point of view.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Likewise, AMOs which do not actually change the value in memory (e.g., an AMOMAX for which the value in rs2 is smaller than the value currently in memory) are still semantically considered store operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Microarchitectures that attempt to implement silent stores must take care to ensure that the memory model is still obeyed, particularly in cases such as RSW which tend to be incompatible with silent stores. * Writes may be merged (i.e., two consecutive writes to the same address may be merged) or subsumed (i.e., the earlier of two back-to-back writes to the same address may be elided) as long as the resulting behavior does not otherwise violate the memory model semantics.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: The question of write subsumption can be understood from the following example:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: .Write subsumption litmus test, allowed execution [float="center",align="center",cols=".^1a,.^1a",frame="none",grid="none",options="noheader"] | | ! 2+!Hart 0 2+^!Hart 1 2+!li t1, 3 2+^!li t3, 2 ! !li t2, 1 ! ! !(a) !sw t1,0(s0) !(d) !lw a0,0(s1) !(b) !fence w, w !(e) !sw a0,0(s0) !(c) !sw t2,0(s1) !(f) !sw t3,0(s0) ! | ! //a! graphviz::images/graphviz/litmussubsumption.txt[] a! image::graphviz/litmussubsumption.png[] ! |
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: As written, if the load (d) reads value 1, then (a) must precede (f) in the global memory order:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: (a) precedes (c) in the global memory order because of rule 4 * (c) precedes (d) in the global memory order because of the Load Value axiom * (d) precedes (e) in the global memory order because of rule 10 * (e) precedes (f) in the global memory order because of rule 1
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: In other words the final value of the memory location whose address is in `s0` must be 2 (the value written by the store (f)) and cannot be 3 (the value written by the store (a)).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: A very aggressive microarchitecture might erroneously decide to discard (e), as (f) supersedes it, and this may in turn lead the microarchitecture to break the now-eliminated dependency between (d) and (f) (and hence also between (a) and (f)).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: This would violate the memory model rules, and hence it is forbidden.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: Write subsumption may in other cases be legal, if for example there were no data dependency between (d) and (e).
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

sentences.text: We expect that any or all of the following possible future extensions would be compatible with the RVWMO memory model:
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines > Possible Future Extensions

sentences.text: "V" vector ISA extensions * "J" JIT extension * Native encodings for load and store opcodes with aq and rl set * Fences limited to certain addresses * Cache write-back/flush/invalidate/etc.instructions
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines > Possible Future Extensions

sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW

sentences.text: There is a known discrepancy between the operational and axiomatic specifications within the family of mixed-size RSW variants shown in -.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW

sentences.text: To address this, we may choose to add something like the following new PPO rule: Memory operation a precedes memory operation b in preserved program order (and hence also in the global memory order) if a precedes b in program order, a and b both access regular main memory (rather than I/O regions), a is a load, b is a store, there is a load m between a and b, there is a byte x that both a and m read, there is no store between a and m that writes to x, and m precedes b in PPO.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW

sentences.text: In other words, in herd syntax, we may choose to add `(po-loc & rsw);ppo;[W]` to PPO.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW

sentences.text: Many implementations will already enforce this ordering naturally.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW

sentences.text: As such, even though this rule is not official, we recommend that implementers enforce it nevertheless in order to ensure forwards compatibility with the possible future addition of this rule to RVWMO.
sentences.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW
